sutta: buddha said monks there are the 5 powers of a learner what 5 the power of faith sadha of conscience or conscientiousness of remorse of diligence virya and of wisdom panna and what monks is the power of faith sadtha here in monks the aryan disciple has faith and believes in the enlightenment of the tata gata that of a truth he is the exalted 1 arahan fully enlightened replete with practice and knowledge well gone 1 world noah incomparable tamer of tameable men teacher of devas and men the buddha exalted 1 this monks is called the power of faith stop here for a moment this word sadda which we translate here as faith can also mean confidence trust or unshakeable faith and this is not blind faith it means having understood the dhamma having understood the teachings then you have faith that the buddha is enlightened so to have faith it means that you must learn you must hear the sutas either study them read them or hear the sutas and understand and only when you have understood now how logical is the buddha's teaching and the buddha in fact now if you study further on you find the buddha said not to believe blindly even the buddha's words himself not to take it on faith but to investigate and to find out whether they are reasonable and slowly from understanding them then you have this sadha unshakable faith and the buddha continue and what monks is the power of conscience or conscientiousness pali word is hearing here in monks the aryan disciple is conscientious he feels conscientious scruple when acting wrongly in deed word and thought on entering on evil and wrong states this monks is called the power of conscientiousness and what monks is the power of remorse o tapa here in monks the aryan disciple is remorseful he is remorseful when acting wrongly in deed word and thought on entering on evil and wrong states this monks is called the power of remorse stop here for a while these 2 conditions these 2 uh powers uh learners powers called hiri and otapa uh they are translated sometimes differently in different books like um and they are very important conditions for someone new a learner on the spiritual path the first 1 conscience is like a sense of shame fear to do wrong that's the meaning of he really before a person does wrong he has this fear of doing wrong this sense of shame that will stop him from doing wrong that's why we translate it as conscience the second 1 is remorse that means the ability to feel remorse at having done wrong it's also a sense of shame now at having done wrong so these 2 things combine now 1 is the fear to do something wrong and the second 1 is after doing wrong or having that remorse and the ability to feel shameful or remorseful about it there are some people without this remorse and i even have after having done wrong they don't feel remorseful and if you don't feel remorseful then you do the wrong again and again then the buddha continue and what monks is the power of diligence viriya here in monks the aryan disciple abiding in active energy puts away all unskillful states and takes to skillful states steadfast and strenuous he shirks not the burden of skillful states this monks is called the power of diligence i'll stop here for a moment our buddha's teachings is always concerned with skillful living skillful living not unskillful living to live our life in a skillful way so that we can avoid unnecessary pain and suffering for example if you drink and drive and then you would end up with an accident that could have been avoided if you did not drink and drive at the same time so in the same way if we understand karma we know that if we do something wrong it's gonna result in suffering for us later so we avoid it from understanding of karma how or what what action leads to what result so if we know that then we avoid unskillful states skillful states meaning states associated with greed hatred and delusion or illusion and that also means like conceit [Music] jealousy anger etc and accentual desire ill will sloth and topper restlessness doubt etc whereas skillful states is the uh reverse the opposite states of mind connected with renunciation with a loving kindness with wisdom humility etc and then the buddha continued and what monks is the power of wisdom here in monks the aryan disciple has wisdom he is endowed with wisdom into the nature of growth and decay with aryan penetration of the way to the utter destruction of dukkha or illness or suffering this monks is called the power of wisdom these monks are the 5 powers of a learner wherefore monks you should train yourselves thus we will become possessed of the learner's powers thus must you train yourselves monks so the last 1 is wisdom wisdom here is explained as being endowed with wisdom into the nature of growth and decay the nature of growth arising and decaying is the nature of the world if you look into everything in the world there is growth arising and decay even and something as hard as a metal uh for example an iron bridge in those days they used to make bailey bridges iron bridges and even though it's the whole bridge is made of iron so strong after many years you find you to rush the action of the weather rain and sunshine et cetera 1 day the whole bridge might collapse and even you can see such a hard thing as iron can collapse what more our body our body that's so soft so that is the nature of everything in this world because the nature of this world is impermanent so if we have wisdom we realize that everything arises and passes away due to conditions that is the principle of dependent origination dependent arising how things arise and pass away all due to conditions and this person with wisdom can see the way to the utter destruction of dukkha what is another characteristic of this world sometimes it's translated as unsatisfactoriness sometimes as suffering um it means like uh growing old is suffering is dukkha uh becoming sick is dukkha dying is dukkha not to get what you want is dukkha to be separated from your loved ones is dukkha to be near to those that you hate is also dukkha in short the buddha said the 5 aggregates of clinging that which we associate with the self the body feelings perception volition and consciousness all that is dukkha so these are the 5 powers of a learner and these 5 powers are very important for somebody on the spiritual path without these 5 learners powers now we cannot progress the first 1 is to have confidence in the buddha's teachings because the buddha's teachings although we call it buddhism it is just a brand we put buddha's teachings the dhamma is something actually we don't have to put a brand because it is reality itself it is truth about existence and because some of it can be found in other religions but uh the buddha as you can you probably heard in 1 of the sutas where the buddha said that the whether the buddha appears or the buddha does not appear the fixed law of dharma applies to all of us it has always been there just that the buddha is enlightened and he shows us shows it to us something that is so obvious that we have not seen it he shows it to us then we realize the nature of existence the nature of the world and how our existence is unsatisfactory and how we can get out of suffering so these are the 5 learner's powers then the next utah is
